name: wsgi-intercept

metadata.name: wsgi-intercept
metadata.summary: wsgi_intercept installs a WSGI application in place of a real URI for testing.

documentation:
Installs a WSGI application in place of a real host for testing.

# Introduction

Testing a WSGI application sometimes involves starting a server at a
local host and port, then pointing your test code to that address.
Instead, this library lets you intercept calls to any specific host/port
combination and redirect them into a [WSGI application] importable by
your test program. Thus, you can avoid spawning multiple processes or
threads to test your Web app.

# Supported Libaries

`wsgi_intercept` works with a variety of HTTP clients in Python 2.7,
3.7 and beyond, and in pypy.

- urllib2
- urllib.request
- httplib
- http.client
- httplib2
- requests
- urllib3 (\<2.0.0, urllib3 2 support is in progress)

# How Does It Work?

`wsgi_intercept` works by replacing `httplib.HTTPConnection` with a
subclass, `wsgi_intercept.WSGI_HTTPConnection`. This class then
redirects specific server/port combinations into a WSGI application by
emulating a socket. If no intercept is registered for the host and port
requested, those requests are passed on to the standard handler.

The easiest way to use an intercept is to import an appropriate subclass
of `~wsgi_intercept.interceptor.Interceptor` and use that as a
context manager over web requests that use the library associated with
the subclass. For example:

```
import httplib2
from wsgi_intercept.interceptor import Httplib2Interceptor
from mywsgiapp import app

def load_app():
    return app

http = httplib2.Http()
with Httplib2Interceptor(load_app, host='example.com', port=80) as url:
    response, content = http.request('%s%s' % (url, '/path'))
    assert response.status == 200
```

The interceptor class may aslo be used directly to install intercepts.
See the module documentation for more information.

Older versions required that the functions `add_wsgi_intercept(host,
port, app_create_fn, script_name='')` and `remove_wsgi_intercept(host,port)`
be used to specify which URLs should be redirected into what applications.
These methods are still available, but the `Interceptor` classes are likely
easier to use for most use cases.

:::{note}
`app_create_fn` is a *function object* returning a WSGI
application; `script_name` becomes `SCRIPT_NAME` in the WSGI
app's environment, if set.
:::

:::{note}
If `http_proxy` or `https_proxy` is set in the environment
this can cause difficulties with some of the intercepted libraries.
If requests or urllib is being used, these will raise an exception
if one of those variables is set.
:::

:::{note}
If `wsgi_intercept.STRICT_RESPONSE_HEADERS` is set to `True`
then response headers sent by an application will be checked to
make sure they are of the type `str` native to the version of
Python, as required by pep 3333. The default is `False` (to
preserve backwards compatibility)
:::

# Install

```
pip install -U wsgi_intercept
```

# Packages Intercepted

Unfortunately each of the HTTP client libraries use their own specific
mechanism for making HTTP call-outs, so individual implementations are
needed. At this time there are implementations for `httplib2`,
`urllib3` (\<2.0.0) and `requests` in both Python 2 and 3, `urllib2` and
`httplib` in Python 2 and `urllib.request` and `http.client`
in Python 3.

If you are using Python 2 and need support for a different HTTP
client, require a version of `wsgi_intercept<0.6`. Earlier versions
include support for `webtest`, `webunit` and `zope.testbrowser`.

The best way to figure out how to use interception is to inspect
[the tests]. More comprehensive documentation available upon
request.

# History

Pursuant to Ian Bicking's ["best Web testing framework"] post, Titus
Brown put together an [in-process HTTP-to-WSGI interception mechanism]
for his own Web testing system, twill. Because the mechanism is pretty
generic -- it works at the httplib level -- Titus decided to try adding
it into all of the *other* Python Web testing frameworks.

The Python 2 version of wsgi-intercept was the result. Kumar McMillan
later took over maintenance.

The current version is tested with Python 2.7, 3.5-3.11, and pypy and pypy3.
It was assembled by [Chris Dent]. Testing and documentation improvements
from [Sasha Hart].

# Project Home

This project lives on [GitHub]. Please submit all bugs, patches,
failing tests, et cetera using the Issue Tracker.

Additional documentation is available on [Read The Docs].

["best web testing framework"]: http://blog.ianbicking.org/best-of-the-web-app-test-frameworks.html
[chris dent]: https://github.com/cdent
[github]: http://github.com/cdent/wsgi-intercept
[in-process http-to-wsgi interception mechanism]: http://www.advogato.org/person/titus/diary.html?start=119
[read the docs]: http://wsgi-intercept.readthedocs.org/en/latest/
[sasha hart]: https://github.com/sashahart
[the tests]: https://github.com/cdent/wsgi-intercept/tree/master/test
[wsgi application]: http://www.python.org/peps/pep-3333.html

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.urllib_intercept
documentation:
Intercept HTTP connections that use urllib.request (Python 3)
aka urllib2 (Python 2).

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.urllib_intercept.WSGI_HTTPHandler
documentation:
Override the default HTTPHandler class with one that uses the
WSGI_HTTPConnection class to open HTTP URLs.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.urllib_intercept.WSGI_HTTPSHandler
documentation:
Override the default HTTPSHandler class with one that uses the
WSGI_HTTPConnection class to open HTTPS URLs.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept._urllib3
documentation: Common code of urllib3 (<2.0.0) and requests intercepts.

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_urllib.test_proxy_handling
documentation: Like requests, urllib gets confused about proxy early on.

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_httplib2.test_proxy_handling
documentation: Proxy has no impact.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_module_interceptor
documentation: Test intercepting a full module with interceptor.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_interceptor
documentation:
Tests of using the context manager style.

The context manager is based on the InterceptFixture used in gabbi.

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_interceptor.test_interceptor_instance
documentation: Base

documentation: http_lib

documentation: Httplib2

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_interceptor.test_requests_interceptor_host
documentation: Requests

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_interceptor.test_urllib3_interceptor_host
documentation: urllib3

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_interceptor.test_urllib_interceptor_host
documentation: urllib

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_http_client.test_proxy_handling
documentation: Proxy variable no impact.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.wsgi_app
documentation: Simple WSGI applications for testing.

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.wsgi_app.simple_app
documentation: Simplest possible application object

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_wsgiref.test_wsgiref
documentation:
General test that the wsgiref server behaves.

This mostly confirms that environ handling is correct in both
python2 and 3.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_response_headers
documentation:
Test response header validations.

Response headers are supposed to be bytestrings and some servers,
notably will experience an error if they are given headers with
the wrong form. Since wsgi-intercept is standing in as a server,
it should behave like one on this front. At the moment it does
not. There are tests for how it delivers request headers, but
not the other way round. Let's write some tests to fix that.

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_response_headers.test_header_app
documentation:
Make sure the header apps returns headers.

Many libraries normalize headers to strings so we're not
going to get exact matches.

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_response_headers.test_encoding_violation
documentation: If the header is unicode we expect boom.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.tests.test_response_headers.HeaderApp
documentation: A simple app that returns whatever headers we give it.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.requests_intercept
documentation:
Intercept HTTP connections that use
`requests <http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/>`_.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.urllib3_intercept
documentation:
Intercept HTTP connections that use
`urllib3 <https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/>`_.

Note that currently only urllib3 <2.0.0 is supported. 2.0.0 support
is in progress.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.httplib2_intercept
documentation:
Intercept HTTP connections that use
`httplib2 <https://github.com/jcgregorio/httplib2>`_.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.interceptor
documentation: Context manager based WSGI interception.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.interceptor.Interceptor
documentation:
A convenience class over the guts of wsgi_intercept.

An Interceptor subclass provides a clean entry point to the wsgi_intercept
functionality in two ways: by encapsulating the interception addition and
removal in methods and by providing a context manager that automates the
process of addition and removal.

Each Interceptor subclass is associated with a specific http library.

Each class may be passed a url or a host and a port. If no args are passed
a hostname will be automatically generated and the resulting url will be
returned by the context manager.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.interceptor.HttpClientInterceptor
documentation: Interceptor for httplib and http.client.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.interceptor.Httplib2Interceptor
documentation: Interceptor for httplib2.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.interceptor.RequestsInterceptor
documentation: Interceptor for requests.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.interceptor.Urllib3Interceptor
documentation: Interceptor for requests.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.interceptor.UrllibInterceptor
documentation: Interceptor for urllib2 and urllib.request.

modules.name: wsgi_intercept.http_client_intercept
documentation: Intercept HTTP connections that use httplib (Py2) or http.client (Py3).

modules.name: wsgi_intercept
documentation:
Installs a WSGI application in place of a real host for testing.

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.add_wsgi_intercept
documentation:
Add a WSGI intercept call for host:port, using the app returned
by app_create_fn with a SCRIPT_NAME of 'script_name' (default '').

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.remove_wsgi_intercept
documentation:
Remove the WSGI intercept call for (host, port).  If no arguments are
given, removes all intercepts

functions.name: wsgi_intercept.make_environ
documentation:
Take 'inp' as if it were HTTP-speak being received on host:port,
and parse it into a WSGI-ok environment dictionary.  Return the
dictionary.

Set 'SCRIPT_NAME' from the 'script_name' input, and, if present,
remove it from the beginning of the PATH_INFO variable.
make_environ: behave like a Web server.  Take in 'input', and behave
as if you're bound to 'host' and 'port'; build an environment dict
for the WSGI app.
This is where the magic happens, folks.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.WSGIAppError
documentation:
An exception that wraps any Exception raised by the WSGI app
that is called. This is done for two reasons: it ensures that
intercepted libraries (such as requests) which use exceptions
to trigger behaviors are not interfered with by exceptions from
the WSGI app. It also helps to define a solid boundary, akin
to the network boundary between server and client, in the
testing environment.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.wsgi_fake_socket

methods.name: wsgi_intercept.wsgi_fake_socket.makefile
wsgi_intercept.wsgi_fake_socket.sendall
documentation: Save all the traffic to self.inp.

Handle HTTP traffic and stuff into a WSGI application object instead.

Note that this class assumes:

 1. 'makefile' is called (by the response class) only after all of the
    data has been sent to the socket by the request class;
 2. non-persistent (i.e. non-HTTP/1.1) connections.
fake socket for WSGI intercept stuff.

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.WSGI_HTTPConnection

methods.name: wsgi_intercept.WSGI_HTTPConnection.get_app
documentation: Return the app object for the given (host, port).

methods.name: wsgi_intercept.WSGI_HTTPConnection.connect
documentation:
Override the connect() function to intercept calls to certain
host/ports.

If no app at host/port has been registered for interception then
a normal HTTPConnection is made.

Intercept all traffic to certain hosts & redirect into a WSGI
application object.
WSGI_HTTPConnection

classes.name: wsgi_intercept.WSGI_HTTPSConnection

methods.name: wsgi_intercept.WSGI_HTTPSConnection.get_app
documentation: Return the app object for the given (host, port).

methods.name: wsgi_intercept.WSGI_HTTPSConnection.connect
documentation:
Override the connect() function to intercept calls to certain
host/ports.

If no app at host/port has been registered for interception then
a normal HTTPSConnection is made.

Intercept all traffic to certain hosts & redirect into a WSGI
application object.
WSGI_HTTPSConnection
